runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Eissa and Hoynes on labor supply of married women/secondary earners

runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that secondary earners, especially married women, tend to have higher labor-supply substitution elasticities than primary earners, but microsimulation implementations often use moderate values rather than the largest empirical estimates. citations: Eissa and Hoynes labor supply/tax studies on married women
Heim estimates of married female labor supply elasticities
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz review of taxable income and labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that secondary earners in U.S. household labor-supply settings, especially married women, are usually assigned noticeably larger substitution elasticities than primary earners, often in the low-to-moderate range rather than near zero.
